talk_title: Biomarker‑guided precision prevention and chemoprevention of HCC

speaker.name: Yujin Hosoda
speaker.affiliation: University of Texas Southwestern Medical Center, Director of Liver Tumor Translational Research

biomarkers.name: Prognostic Liver Signature (PLS)
biomarkers.finding: High PLS risk score predicts higher 5‑year HCC incidence; risk score reduced in ~80% of antiviral‑cured patients

biomarkers.name: Phospho‑EGFR
biomarkers.finding: Suppressed at 25 mg erlotinib (one‑sixth therapeutic dose) in window trial liver biopsies

biomarkers.name: Risk signature modulation
biomarkers.finding: Significant down‑modulation observed at ≥50 mg erlotinib with no grade ≥3 adverse events

models.name: diet‑induced fibrosis‑cirrhosis‑HCC model
models.genotype_or_key_features: progressive fibrosis → cirrhosis → early HCC; high‑risk gene expression signature

models.name: Texas prospective cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: n>2000 cirrhotic patients, longitudinal PLS validation

models.name: Phase 1 window trial (cirrhosis patients undergoing liver resection)
models.genotype_or_key_features: dose escalation erlotinib 25‑75 mg, paired pre‑/post‑treatment biopsies

clinical_data.population: cirrhotic patients undergoing elective liver surgery

clinical_data.value: significant reduction at 50 mg and 75 mg
mild rash at 75 mg, resolved after drug stop
dose‑dependent decrease, significant for small nodules only

MOA_summary: Hosoda demonstrated that a liver‑tissue risk signature (PLS) can be modulated by targeting EGFR signaling in myofibroblasts with low‑dose erlotinib, leading to dose‑dependent suppression of phospho‑EGFR, reduction of early HCC nodules, and improvement of the risk signature; similar modulation is observed with lipophilic statins, bariatric surgery, and GLP‑1 agonists, supporting the signature as a surrogate endpoint for chemoprevention trials.
key_findings: Risk‑stratified HCC screening using the PLS risk signature improves cost‑effectiveness and predicts incident HCC in >2,000 US patients and a 1,300‑patient NIH cohort.
Low‑dose erlotinib (25‑75 mg) suppresses phospho‑EGFR in cirrhotic liver tissue, with risk‑signature improvement at ≥50 mg and no grade ≥3 toxicity.
Erlotinib reduces early HCC nodules in a rat fibrosis model in a dose‑dependent manner, but has minimal effect on larger established tumors due to poor tumor cell uptake.
Lipophilic statins (atorvastatin) and bariatric surgery improve the PLS risk signature, correlating with lower long‑term HCC incidence.
The EGFR‑driven myofibroblast signature can serve as a surrogate endpoint for future chemoprevention trials (ECHO‑B, CATCH‑B, TOSS).
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Erlotinib fails to shrink larger HCC nodules because the drug is poorly taken up by tumor cells, limiting activity to early, myofibroblast‑driven stages.
Only ~80% of antiviral‑cured patients show risk‑signature reduction, indicating heterogeneity in chemoprevention response.

combination_strategies.rationale: Potential synergy by combining EGFR inhibition in myofibroblasts with checkpoint blockade to enhance T‑cell infiltration
combination_strategies.phenotype: proposed enhanced early‑stage tumor control without added toxicity

open_questions: What molecular features determine why only a subset of patients achieve risk‑signature modulation with antiviral or statin therapy?
Can the PLS signature predict response to other targeted agents beyond EGFR inhibition?

talk_title: Personalized care in advanced HCC: gamma‑delta T cells, liver decompensation AI model, and genomics‑driven targeted therapy

speaker.name: Manon Allaire
speaker.affiliation: La Pitié‑Salpêtrière Hospital, Paris, France; Inserm Centre de Recherche des Cordeliers

biomarkers.name: Gamma‑delta T cell signature
biomarkers.finding: Higher baseline gamma‑delta T cell levels associated with disease control at 6 months and significantly longer PFS/OS in atezolizumab‑bevacizumab‑treated HCC (n=31)

biomarkers.name: Interferon‑γ level
biomarkers.finding: Baseline serum IFN‑γ higher in responders versus non‑responders in the same cohort

biomarkers.name: Portal hypertension (CT‑derived HepatoSAJ model)
biomarkers.finding: Model predicts high risk of liver decompensation (AUC ≈ 0.84) and associated 30% 1‑year decompensation rate, correlating with median OS 6 months vs 24 months without decompensation

models.name: Prospective French atezo‑bev cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: n=31 advanced HCC patients, blood RNA‑seq and cytokine profiling

models.name: Multicentric French real‑world cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: n=180 advanced HCC, median OS 23 months, 50% portal hypertension

models.name: 500‑patient CT‑AI cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: Baseline CT used to train HepatoSAJ model for decompensation risk

models.name: Molecular tumor board cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: n=103 sequenced liver tumors, 65% with targetable alteration

clinical_data.value: significantly higher in high gamma‑delta T cell group
longer in high gamma‑delta T cell signature (median not specified)
median 23 months in real‑world French cohort (n=180)
30% at 1 year in AI‑predicted high‑risk group
32% of treated patients achieved disease control (median PFS 12 months vs 2 months)

MOA_summary: Allaire identified a blood‑based gamma‑delta T cell signature that predicts response to atezolizumab‑bevacizumab in HCC, demonstrated that CT‑derived AI modeling (HepatoSAJ) can stratify patients at high risk of portal‑hypertension‑driven decompensation, and showed that genomics‑driven molecular tumor boards enable targeted therapy for ~65% of liver cancer patients, yielding disease control in one‑third of treated cases.
key_findings: High baseline gamma‑delta T cell levels in blood correlate with disease control and longer PFS/OS in atezo‑bev‑treated HCC.
Interferon‑γ is elevated at baseline in responders, supporting a Th1‑type immune milieu.
The HepatoSAJ CT‑AI model predicts liver decompensation risk (AUC ≈ 0.84) better than conventional clinical scores.
Portal hypertension identified by AI is a stronger predictor of mortality than tumor‑related factors in real‑world HCC cohorts.
Genomic profiling revealed targetable alterations in 65% of liver cancer patients; genotype‑matched therapy achieved disease control in 32% with a median PFS of 12 months.

combination_strategies.rationale: Gamma‑delta T cells produce IFN‑γ; combining with checkpoint blockade may amplify Th1 response
combination_strategies.phenotype: proposed improved response rates in atezo‑bev refractory patients

combination_strategies.rationale: Reduce portal hypertension‑driven decompensation in AI‑high‑risk patients

open_questions: Does the gamma‑delta T cell signature reflect tumor‑infiltrating cells or systemic immunity, and can it be modulated therapeutically?
Can the HepatoSAJ model be integrated with longitudinal lab data to further improve decompensation prediction?
What are the mechanisms of resistance in patients with targetable alterations who still progress on matched therapy?

cited_papers: Zucman‑Rossi et al. Nat Rev Cancer 2023
